title: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 5: The Doctrine of the Word - Part II
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 5 - The Doctrine of the Word - Part II - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The New Testament's Witness to the Old Testament
subpoints: New Testament authors cited the Old Testament as the final word of God in doctrinal disputes
Old Testament quotations are introduced with 'God says,' 'the Holy Spirit says,' or 'it is written,' equating human authorship with divine origin
The author of Hebrews quotes Psalms attributed to David and attributes them to the Holy Spirit (Hebrews 3:7; 4:7)
The Jerusalem Council in Acts 15 settled the Gentile question by citing Amos 9, showing the Old Testament as authoritative final word

outline.title: What Jesus Believed About the Old Testament
subpoints: Jesus used the Old Testament continuously across all its historical sections, from Genesis through the Prophets and Writings
In the Sermon on the Mount, 'You have heard it said' refers to popular misinterpretations, not the Old Testament itself; 'It is written' introduces the authoritative text
Jesus' use of 'it is written' (perfect tense: written once, stands forever) shows He regarded the Scripture as permanently binding
Even in temptation, Jesus did not rebuke Satan but quoted Scripture; His reference from Abel to Zechariah affirms the entire Hebrew canon

outline.title: What the Apostles Believed About Their Own Writings
subpoints: Paul instructed churches to circulate his letters as authoritative (Colossians 4:16)
Paul explicitly told the Thessalonians his words were the Word of God, not merely human words (1 Thessalonians 2:13)
Paul demanded discipline for those who disregarded his letter (2 Thessalonians 3:14)
Peter classified Paul's writings alongside 'the other scriptures' (2 Peter 3:16)

outline.title: The Doctrine of Inspiration
subpoints: 2 Timothy 3:16: all Scripture is 'breathed out by God' - the image is of speaking, God's breath forming words
2 Peter 1:21: men were 'carried along by the Holy Spirit' - like wind filling sails, the Spirit propelled the writers
Inspiration is both plenary (all of Scripture) and verbal (every word), applying to the original languages
The preparation of the writers - their birth, training, conversion, and circumstances - was all sovereignly ordered by God so that the exact words He wanted were produced

outline.title: Inadequate Views of Inspiration and the Verbal Nature of Scripture
subpoints: Ordinary inspiration (like Shakespeare), limited inspiration (only some parts), dynamic inspiration (thoughts but not words), and dictation theory are all rejected
Jesus affirmed the inspiration of individual letters and strokes of the pen, not merely thoughts or passages (Matthew 5:18)
Paul's argument in Galatians 3 rests on the difference between 'seed' (singular) and 'seeds' (plural) - showing every word matters
Inspiration refers to the text, not the author; not everything Paul said or did was inspired, only what is preserved in Scripture

summary:
This session continues the Foundations series' treatment of the Doctrine of the Word, examining how both the New Testament and Jesus Himself viewed the Old Testament, followed by an introduction to the doctrine of inspiration. Pastor Lyro begins by demonstrating that the New Testament authors treated the Old Testament as the final authority in disputes. Passages attributed to David in the Psalms are introduced by the author of Hebrews as the words of the Holy Spirit, showing that in the apostolic mind, human authorship and divine origin were one and the same. The Jerusalem Council settled its controversy by citing Amos 9, illustrating the Old Testament's decisive authority.

The central section examines what Jesus believed about Scripture. Lyro argues carefully that in the Sermon on the Mount, the formula 'You have heard it said' refers to the popular misinterpretations of the Pharisees, not to the Old Testament itself; the contrasting 'But I say to you' is actually a restoration of the original intent of the Law. Jesus consistently cited Scripture with 'it is written' - a perfect tense verb in Greek indicating a past action whose results stand permanently. When tempted by Satan, Jesus did not rebuke him supernaturally but quoted Scripture. His reference from Abel (Genesis) to Zechariah (2 Chronicles, the last book of the Hebrew canon) implicitly endorsed the entire Old Testament canon.

Lyro then surveys what the apostles believed about their own writings: Paul instructed churches to share and obey his letters as divine authority, the Thessalonian letters frame Paul's words as God's Word, and Peter classifies Paul's letters alongside Scripture. The session closes with the classic doctrine of inspiration drawn from 2 Timothy 3:16 and 2 Peter 1:21 - all Scripture is God-breathed, carried along by the Spirit as a ship is carried by wind. Inspiration is both plenary (all of Scripture) and verbal (every word), and extends to the original languages. Common inadequate views of inspiration are surveyed and rejected.
transcript:
We are continuing our Foundations series looking at the doctrine of the Word of God. Hopefully our faith is grounded in the Scriptures, and to know that the Bible is the Word of God is an important thing. Last week we looked at what revelation is and the Old Testament’s witness to itself — how the Old Testament saw itself as the Word of God, as authoritative. Today we continue with the New Testament’s witness to the Old Testament.

This is important because people throughout history have decided to take some parts of the Bible and not others. Thomas Jefferson literally cut up a Bible and glued together only the parts he wanted — the Jefferson Bible. Today the Jesus Seminar sits around a table and votes on which words of Jesus are authentic, marking them with red, black, or shaded stars. But the Bible presents itself as an all-or-nothing proposition: either the whole thing is the revelation of God, or none of it is.

## The New Testament’s Witness to the Old Testament

Throughout the New Testament, the writers quoted the Old Testament to prove the point they were making. When teaching doctrine, when teaching about Christ or about life, they would quote or allude to the Old Testament — and that was considered the final word. The Old Testament was the Bible of the apostolic church. As Paul went around preaching the gospel, he preached it from the Old Testament.

Notice how the New Testament introduces Old Testament quotations: *God says*, *the Holy Spirit says*, *the Scripture says*, *as it is written*. Often you find the New Testament saying *the Holy Spirit said* where the Old Testament said *David said*. The New Testament equates the human author’s words with the words of the Holy Spirit.

For example, turn to Hebrews 1:5. The author asks: *"For to which of the angels did He ever say, ‘You are My Son, today I have begotten You’?"* — quoting Psalm 110, a psalm of David. Then in chapter 3:7, he says:

> *"Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says: ‘Today, if you hear His voice, do not harden your hearts’”* (Hebrews 3:7)

— quoting Psalm 95. And in chapter 4:7 he says *"saying in David"* and quotes the same Psalm again. So the author of Hebrews attributes what David wrote to the Holy Spirit. The apostolic church believed that what they had in the Old Testament — though human-authored — was ultimately the word of God.

Look also at Acts 15. There was a great debate about what it meant to be a Gentile Christian — whether Gentiles had to become fully Jewish to be Christians. James the elder settles the issue by quoting Amos 9. That’s it — the issue is settled. *The Bible says it.* That shows the attitude of the apostolic church to Scripture.

And the classic passage, 2 Timothy 3:16:

> *"All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness."* (2 Timothy 3:16)

In the same epistle Paul quotes words from Luke and from Deuteronomy, calling them both *the Scriptures* — equating the Old Testament and the New Testament writings as Scripture.

## What Jesus Believed About the Old Testament

The Lord Jesus is the most important witness to Scripture, because He is the final revelation of God — the second person of the Trinity, the prophet of all prophets. His attitude to Scripture should be ours.

Throughout the gospel accounts, Jesus used the entire Old Testament to teach. In fact, the entire Old Testament history could be reconstructed just through the teaching of Jesus Christ. He quotes from Genesis 1 and Genesis 2 in Matthew 19 — which is significant because liberal theology claims two different authors wrote those chapters. But Jesus says *Moses said*, and then quotes both. He talks about Abel and the flood (Matthew 4, 24), the destruction of Sodom, Lot’s wife, Abraham’s offering of Isaac, Moses and the burning bush, manna in the wilderness, the serpent in the wilderness, David eating the consecrated bread, Solomon in his glory, the queen of Sheba, Elijah and the widow of Zarephath (1 Kings 17), Naaman the leper (2 Kings 5), and Jonah and the fish. Jesus saw all of these as real events with theological meaning, to be believed exactly as they happened — not merely as allegory.

In Matthew 23:35, Jesus says: *"from the blood of innocent Abel to the blood of Zechariah the son of Barachiah, whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar."* Abel is from Genesis — the first book of the Hebrew Bible. Zechariah is from 2 Chronicles — the last book of the Hebrew Bible in its first-century ordering. By citing from the first to the last, Jesus endorsed the entire canon of the Hebrew Scriptures.

Jesus continually pointed to the text with the expression *it is written*. In the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7), when Jesus says *"You have heard it said,"* He is not referring to the Old Testament — He is referring to what the teachers of the law were teaching, the popular misinterpretations of the Pharisees. When He says *"But I say to you,"* He is actually restoring the original intent of the Law. He did not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it (Matthew 5:17).

The expression *it is written* is in the perfect tense in the original Greek — meaning: it was written in the past and the result stands today as it was written then. It is a past action whose result continues permanently. The Lord of Heaven, when tempted by the devil, did not rebuke Satan — He quoted Scripture.

Turn to Mark 7:9. Jesus says:

> *"All too well you reject the commandment of God, that you may keep your tradition. For Moses said, ‘Honor your father and your mother’; and, ‘He who curses his father or mother, let him be put to death.’ But you say, ‘If a man says to his father or mother, “Whatever profit you might have received from me is Corban”’ — then you no longer let him do anything for his father or his mother, making the word of God of no effect through your tradition which you have handed down."* (Mark 7:9-13)

One of the worst things the Pharisees were doing was placing man-made traditions above Scripture. That was devastating to Jesus, because it is in the Scriptures that you find about Him.

## What the Apostles Believed About Their Own Writings

Did the apostles think what they were saying was the Word of God? We often treat the apostles casually — as if their writings are optional suggestions. But that is not how the apostles saw their own words.

In Colossians 4:16, Paul says: *"When this letter has been read among you, have it also read in the church of the Laodiceans; and see that you also read the letter from Laodicea."* He is not saying this is just for you — send it to other churches, read it to them.

In 1 Thessalonians 2:13, Paul says:

> *"We also thank God constantly for this, that when you received the word of God, which you heard from us, you accepted it not as the word of men but as what it really is, the word of God, which is at work in you believers."* (1 Thessalonians 2:13)

Paul considered what he told the Thessalonians to be the Word of God. In 2 Thessalonians 3:14 he adds: *"If anyone does not obey what we say in this letter, take note of that person, and have nothing to do with him, that he may be ashamed."* Was he looking at 2 Thessalonians as a suggestion? The apostles saw their writings as authoritative, as the Word of God.

Peter confirms this in 2 Peter 3:16, where he refers to Paul’s writings as *scripture* alongside the other Scriptures.

## Scripture as Inspired by God

We move now to Scripture as inspired by God. The doctrine of inspiration claims that every word in the Bible was given to us by God.

The word *inspired* is unfortunate — it literally means *to breathe into.* A more accurate rendering of 2 Timothy 3:16 is *breathed out* — expired, not inspired. God breathed out the Word. What act is that referring to? Speaking. When you speak, air comes out and is shaped by the vocal cords — that is the image: God breathing out His word, just as He breathed life into man.

Note that inspiration applies to the **text**, not the **author**. It is what we have in the Bible that is inspired, not the men themselves. Not everything Paul thought, said, or did was inspired. The clearest example is Peter, who was rebuked by Paul in Galatians 2 for acting contrary to the Scriptures. The apostles were not inspired in their persons — only the writings preserved for us are inspired.

The doctrine of inspiration applies holistically to the whole life of the writer: his birth, his training, his character, his conversion, his sanctification, his ministry, the circumstances that led him to write. Even the problems that caused the apostles to write their letters were orchestrated by God — every epistle in the New Testament was written because of problems within a church or an individual. So even those problems were sovereignly ordered so that at the end, we have the very word God wanted. When Paul finished writing Galatians, those were his words — written in his own style. But they were also exactly the words of God.

As 2 Peter 1:20-21 says:

> *"No prophecy of Scripture comes from someone’s own interpretation. For no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit."* (2 Peter 1:20-21)

The phrase *carried along* is the word for a wind carrying a ship. A skilled sailor can go wherever he wants, as long as there is wind to propel him. He is in control of the boat, yet he can only go where the wind is taking him. That is how the Spirit worked through the writers of Scripture.

Inspiration is both **plenary** (the totality of Scripture — nothing in it is not God’s word) and **verbal** (every word, not just the thoughts). Inspiration applies to the original languages. Our English translations, if accurate, are inspired in a derived sense — but technically inspiration refers to the original texts.

### Inadequate Views of Inspiration

Several inadequate views must be rejected:

- **Ordinary inspiration**: the Bible has genius like Shakespeare. No.
- **Limited inspiration**: only some parts are inspired, while others are not. This was Karl Barth’s Neo-Orthodox position.
- **Dynamic inspiration**: the thoughts are inspired but the exact words are not.
- **Partial inspiration**: the Bible is inspired in sections dealing with religion and doctrine but not in sections dealing with science or history.
- **Dictation theory**: the authors were passive telegraphs through whom God dictated everything word for word. While some parts of Scripture are like this, most are not.

All of these are wrong. The Bible is verbally and plenarily inspired — every word. Jesus affirms this in Matthew 5:18:

> *"For truly, I say to you, until heaven and earth pass away, not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law until all is accomplished."* (Matthew 5:18)

The iota is the smallest letter in the Greek alphabet — and originally would refer to the *yod*, the smallest letter in Hebrew. Not even the smallest letter will pass away. Not even the smallest stroke of a pen. In Psalm 119, divided by the Hebrew alphabet, the letter *dalet* has a vertical piece and a horizontal piece, where the horizontal just barely extends beyond the vertical. That’s the smallest stroke of a pen. Not even that will pass from the Word of God.

And Paul in 1 Corinthians 2 says that we are taught not in human words but in words taught by the Spirit — the very words. In Galatians 3, Paul makes his entire argument about our salvation hinge on the difference between the singular word *seed* and the plural *seeds*. The whole argument — where our salvation hangs — hangs on an *s*. Every word matters.

Let’s pray. Heavenly Father, thank you for your word. We pray that these things would be helpful to us, that we might grow in our understanding and faith in you through your word. Even as we think about the beauty of your word and how you have preserved it for us, drive us to worship you. In Jesus’ name, amen.
